request.question: Hail Flutie was a call made at the game with which managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network calling the action?

title: Norm Clarke
Jonathan Landman
A. Charles Muller
Hail Flutie
Martin Gottlieb
China Economic Information Network
Richard Stolley
Bill Adee
Doug Flutie
Brent Musburger
sentences: Norm Clarke (born 1942 in Terry, Montana) is an American gossip columnist in Las Vegas, Nevada.
He wrote the column "Vegas Confidential" for the "Las Vegas Review-Journal" from 1999 to 2016.
He currently writes the column "Vegas Diary" for the website of the Vegas Stats & Information Network.
His column covers celebrities and near-celebrities and their doings in the bright lights of "Sin City."
Jonathan I. Landman is an American journalist and deputy managing editor at "The New York Times".
Landman became deputy managing editor responsible for digital journalism for "The Times" in August 2005.
He had become assistant managing editor and member of "The Times" masthead in May 2005.
Prior to that, Landman had been acting culture editor from 2004 to 2005.
Previously, he was the assistant managing editor for enterprise from 2003 to 2004.
Landman had been metropolitan editor of "The Times" since 1999 and editor of "The Times"<nowiki>'</nowiki> Week in Review section since 1994.
Before that, he served as deputy editor of the Washington bureau since 1992; assistant editor in Washington from 1991; assistant metropolitan editor from 1990 and assistant national editor from 1989.
Landman joined "The Times" as a copy editor in 1987.
A. Charles Muller (born September 19, 1953) is an academic specializing in Korean Buddhism and East Asian Yogacara, having published numerous books and articles on these topics.
He is a resident of Japan, currently teaching at the University of Tokyo.

He is one of the earliest and most prolific developers of online research resources for the field of Buddhist Studies, being the founder and managing editor of the online Digital Dictionary of Buddhism, the CJKV-English Dictionary, and the H-Buddhism Scholars Information Network, along with having digitized and published numerous reference works.
The Hail Flutie game is a college football game that took place between the Boston College Eagles and the University of Miami Hurricanes on November 23, 1984.
It has been regarded by FOX Sports writer Kevin Hench as among the most memorable moments in sports.
The game is most notable for a last-second Hail Mary pass from quarterback Doug Flutie to wide receiver Gerard Phelan to give Boston College the win.
Miami was the defending national champion and entered the game ranked 12th in the nation.
Boston College was ranked 10th with a record of 8–2 and had already accepted an invitation to the Cotton Bowl Classic at the end of the season.
The game was played at the Miami Orange Bowl, and televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating.
Martin Gottlieb is Assistant Managing Editor/Investigations of "Newsday".
From 2012 to 2016, he was Editor of "The Record" of Bergen County, New Jersey.
Previously, from 2008 to 2011, he was Global Editions Editor of the "New York Times"; in this capacity, he oversaw production of the "International Herald Tribune".
Gottlieb also served as Associate Managing Editor, Deputy Culture Editor and National Project Editor of the "Times".
Gottlieb was also Managing Editor of the "New York Daily News" and Editor-in-Chief of "The Village Voice".
China Economic Information Network (中国经济信息网) is a national information network that provides information and analyses of Chinese national macroeconomic trends.
It is sponsored by the State Information Network.
Richard Brockway Stolley (born October 3, 1928) is an American journalist and magazine editor.
He's most well known for his work at "Time" magazine, which he joined in 1953.
He subsequently held a number of roles at the magazine including report, writer, bureau chief, senior editor and managing editor.

In addition to his work at "Time", he has also been Assistant Managing Editor and Managing Editor of "Life" magazine, the founding Managing Editor of "People" magazine and Director of Special Projects for Time Inc
Bill Adee is Chief Operating Officer for Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN) after joining the network in March 2017.
Adee was formerly executive vice president of digital development and operations for the "Chicago Tribune".
He joined the news organization as its sports editor in the spring of 2002.

He previously worked for the "Chicago Sun-Times", "Los Angeles Daily News", "Akron Beacon Journal" of Akron, Ohio, "The Virginian-Pilot" of Norfolk, Virginia, "The Daily Herald' in Arlington Heights, Illinois and the "News Sun" of Waukegan, Illinois.
Douglas Richard Flutie (born October 23, 1962) is a former quarterback in the National Football League (NFL), Canadian Football League (CFL), and United States Football League (USFL).
He first rose to prominence during his college football career at Boston College, where he received the Heisman Trophy and the Davey O'Brien National Quarterback Award in 1984.
His "Hail Flutie" touchdown pass in a game against Miami on November 23, 1984 (dubbed "The Pass") is considered among the greatest moments in college football and American sports history.
Flutie was selected as the 285th pick in the 11th round of the 1985 NFL Draft by the Los Angeles Rams, making him the lowest drafted Heisman Award winner among those who were drafted.
Flutie played that year for the New Jersey Generals of the upstart USFL, having already signed a five-year $5 million contract with them prior to being drafted by the Rams.
In 1986, he signed with the NFL's Chicago Bears, and later played for the New England Patriots, becoming their starting quarterback in 1988.
Brent Woody Musburger ( ; born May 26, 1939) is an American sportscaster, lead broadcaster and managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN).
He retired in January 2017 from the ESPN and ABC television networks.

Formerly with CBS Sports and one of the original members of their program "The NFL Today", Musburger has also covered the NBA, MLB, NCAA football and basketball, and NASCAR and served as a studio host for games, a play-by-play man, and halftime host.
He has also performed postgame wrap-up segments and covered championship trophy presentations.
He is a member of the Montana Broadcaster's Association Hall of Fame.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Hail Flutie was a call made at the game with which managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network calling the action?
hop1_docs: Vegas Stats & Information Network | VSiN is a national sports gambling news network based in Las Vegas, Nevada.  VSiN’s lead on-air personality is hall of fame broadcaster Brent Musburger, who anchors the weekday program called “My Guys in the Desert," which refers to Musburger’s veiled gambling references during his play-by-play of sports contests in earlier parts of his career.  Musburger left ESPN in January 2017 and joined VSiN.

Brent Musburger | Brent Woody Musburger ( ; born May 26, 1939) is an American sportscaster, lead broadcaster and managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN).  He retired in January 2017 from the ESPN and ABC television networks.  Formerly with CBS Sports and one of the original members of their program "The NFL Today", Musburger has also covered the NBA, MLB, NCAA football and basketball, and NASCAR and served as a studio host for games, a play-by-play man, and halftime host.  He has also performed postgame wrap-up segments and covered championship trophy presentations.  He is a member of the Montana Broadcaster's Association Hall of Fame.

Flutie effect | The Flutie effect or Flutie factor refers to the American phenomenon of having a successful college sports team increase the exposure and prominence of a university.  This is named after Boston College's Doug Flutie, whose successful Hail Mary pass in the 1984 game against the University of Miami clinched a win which allegedly played a large role in the increase in applications to Boston College the following year.

Hail Flutie | The Hail Flutie game is a college football game that took place between the Boston College Eagles and the University of Miami Hurricanes on November 23, 1984.  It has been regarded by FOX Sports writer Kevin Hench as among the most memorable moments in sports.  The game is most notable for a last-second Hail Mary pass from quarterback Doug Flutie to wide receiver Gerard Phelan to give Boston College the win.  Miami was the defending national champion and entered the game ranked 12th in the nation.  Boston College was ranked 10th with a record of 8–2 and had already accepted an invitation to the Cotton Bowl Classic at the end of the season.  The game was played at the Miami Orange Bowl, and televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating.

Stat (system call) | stat() is a Unix system call that returns file attributes about an inode.  The semantics of stat() vary between operating systems.  As an example, Unix command ls uses this system call to retrieve information on files that includes:

Norm Clarke | Norm Clarke (born 1942 in Terry, Montana) is an American gossip columnist in Las Vegas, Nevada.  He wrote the column "Vegas Confidential" for the "Las Vegas Review-Journal" from 1999 to 2016.  He currently writes the column "Vegas Diary" for the website of the Vegas Stats & Information Network.  His column covers celebrities and near-celebrities and their doings in the bright lights of "Sin City."

Bill Adee | Bill Adee is Chief Operating Officer for Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN) after joining the network in March 2017.  Adee was formerly executive vice president of digital development and operations for the "Chicago Tribune".  He joined the news organization as its sports editor in the spring of 2002.  He previously worked for the "Chicago Sun-Times", "Los Angeles Daily News", "Akron Beacon Journal" of Akron, Ohio, "The Virginian-Pilot" of Norfolk, Virginia, "The Daily Herald' in Arlington Heights, Illinois and the "News Sun" of Waukegan, Illinois.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Brent Musburger: passage [2] identifies "Brent Musburger ... managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network (VSiN)" (passage [2]).
  - Hail Flutie telecast: passage [4] states the game was "televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating." (passage [4]).
- Ambiguities:
  - Multiple commentators listed for the CBS telecast: "Brent Musburger," "Ara Parseghian," and "Pat Haden" (passage [4]); only Brent Musburger is labeled a VSiN managing editor in the provided passages. Pages for Parseghian and Haden are not included here.
  - The passages do not explicitly state which commentator "called the action" or uttered the Hail Flutie call.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as in the passages): Brent Musburger.
  - Missing precise fact needed: which commentator (name) actually made the play call or spoke the Hail Flutie line on the CBS broadcast (i.e., explicit attribution of the call).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the "Hail Flutie" page — check lede and sections like "Telecast," "Game summary," or "Broadcast" for attribution of the play call or quoted commentary (search for "called," "call," "Hail Flutie," or direct quote).
  2) Open the "Brent Musburger" page — check lede/infobox and "Broadcasting career" or "Career" sections for mention that he called the play or is credited with the Hail Flutie call.
  3) If needed, fetch pages for "Ara Parseghian" and "Pat Haden" (lede/Career) to see if either is credited with the specific call.
  4) Search for contemporary CBS broadcast transcripts or game audio/video transcripts/quotes (keywords: "Hail Flutie call CBS who said," "who called Hail Mary 1984 CBS 'Hail Flutie'").
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and the specific attribution of the call is confirmed.
response.hop2_query: Who called "Hail Flutie" on the CBS broadcast
hop2_docs: Hail Flutie | The Hail Flutie game is a college football game that took place between the Boston College Eagles and the University of Miami Hurricanes on November 23, 1984.  It has been regarded by FOX Sports writer Kevin Hench as among the most memorable moments in sports.  The game is most notable for a last-second Hail Mary pass from quarterback Doug Flutie to wide receiver Gerard Phelan to give Boston College the win.  Miami was the defending national champion and entered the game ranked 12th in the nation.  Boston College was ranked 10th with a record of 8–2 and had already accepted an invitation to the Cotton Bowl Classic at the end of the season.  The game was played at the Miami Orange Bowl, and televised nationally by CBS, with Brent Musburger, Ara Parseghian, and Pat Haden commentating.

Flutie effect | The Flutie effect or Flutie factor refers to the American phenomenon of having a successful college sports team increase the exposure and prominence of a university.  This is named after Boston College's Doug Flutie, whose successful Hail Mary pass in the 1984 game against the University of Miami clinched a win which allegedly played a large role in the increase in applications to Boston College the following year.

Doug Flutie | Douglas Richard Flutie (born October 23, 1962) is a former quarterback in the National Football League (NFL), Canadian Football League (CFL), and United States Football League (USFL).  He first rose to prominence during his college football career at Boston College, where he received the Heisman Trophy and the Davey O'Brien National Quarterback Award in 1984.  His "Hail Flutie" touchdown pass in a game against Miami on November 23, 1984 (dubbed "The Pass") is considered among the greatest moments in college football and American sports history.  Flutie was selected as the 285th pick in the 11th round of the 1985 NFL Draft by the Los Angeles Rams, making him the lowest drafted Heisman Award winner among those who were drafted.  Flutie played that year for the New Jersey Generals of the upstart USFL, having already signed a five-year $5 million contract with them prior to being drafted by the Rams.  In 1986, he signed with the NFL's Chicago Bears, and later played for the New England Patriots, becoming their starting quarterback in 1988.
Flutie Flakes | Flutie Flakes is the name of a brand of frosted corn flakes breakfast cereal named for American football quarterback Doug Flutie.

CBS Watch | CBS Watch! , also called Watch!  Magazine, is a bi-monthly magazine commissioned by the CBS Corporation which is published by Manifest.  The glossy print magazine, covers celebrity, fashion, beauty, travel, culture and wellness through articles mainly about actors and hosts involved with programming on the CBS broadcast network, as well as CBS affiliates The CW, Showtime, and Pop.  The magazine is published bimonthly in the United States with both newsstand and subscription distribution.  "CBS Watch" has a circulation number of approximately 200,000 copies per edition.  It was created by former CBS Vice President Jeremy Murphy, who left to start his own company (http://wwd.com/business-news/media/jeremy-murphy-cbs-360bespoke-10449703/)

CBS News | CBS News is the news division of American television and radio service CBS.  The president of CBS News is David Rhodes.  CBS News' broadcasts include the "CBS Evening News", "CBS This Morning", news magazine programs "CBS Sunday Morning", "60 Minutes" and "48 Hours", and Sunday morning political affairs program "Face the Nation".  CBS Radio News produces hourly newscasts for hundreds of radio stations, while CBS also operates a 24-hour news network called CBSN, the first live anchored 24-hour streaming news network that is exclusively online and on smart devices.
America Calling | America Calling, sponsored by the Greek War Relief, was broadcast February 8, 1941 on CBS, NBC, independent stations and all of Europe.
response.summary_2: Brent Musburger. Brent Musburger is identified as managing editor at Vegas Stats and Information Network and was one of CBS's commentators for the nationally televised game, but the passages do not specify which commentator actually made the Hail Flutie call.
response.answer: Brent Musburger is the VSiN managing editor and a CBS commentator for the game, but the summaries do not confirm he actually made the "Hail Flutie" call.
